item.title: Pomaleu explains reason behind 30-day deadline
item.description: THE 30-day deadline given to the National Planning and Monitoring Department to transfer certain functions to the National Monitoring and Coordination Authority (NMCA) is because earlier directives were not acted on, says chief secretary Ivan Pomaleu. Pomaleu said the timeframe reflected the urgency attached to the reform.
item.content:encoded:
THE 30-day deadline given to the National Planning and Monitoring Department to transfer certain functions to the National Monitoring and Coordination Authority (NMCA) is because earlier directives were not acted on, says chief secretary Ivan Pomaleu.

Pomaleu said the timeframe reflected the urgency attached to the reform.

The 30-day notice was issued on April 24.

Pomaleu said that it was not the first instruction given to the Department of National Planning.

“Directions issued on July 21, 2025 required the department to prepare a functional transition plan outlining the transfer of monitoring functions, systems and responsibilities to NMCA,” he said.

“(And to) identify and redeploy relevant technical officers in consultation with the Department of Personnel Management, transfer and share monitoring systems, templates, databases and reports, and appoint a dedicated senior liaison officer to work with the NMCA Establishment Team.”

Pomaleu said no substantive action was taken in response to those earlier directions.

He said that the reform was not a sudden proposal, but had been discussed extensively through the Medium Term Development Plan IV, Central Agencies Coordinating Committee deliberations, National Executive Council processes, Reset@50 consultations and subsequent administrative directions.

“The objective is to structurally separate coordination, monitoring and evaluation functions from implementation functions,” he said.

Pomaleu also rejected concerns that the establishment of NMCA would duplicate the work of existing institutions.

“There is no duplication. In fact, the reform is specifically intended to reduce duplication and institutional overlap,” he said.

“Under the new arrangement, DNPM will remain responsible for planning, development programming, budget coordination and administration of the Public Investment Programme.

“The NMCA will focus on monitoring, implementation tracking, coordination, evaluation, compliance oversight, reporting and project performance assurance.

“The NMCA will consolidate and coordinate monitoring resources and functions currently scattered across various agencies rather than creating parallel structures.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Monitoring must be linked to planning, says official
item.description: MONITORING is an important part of government but it must be linked to planning and implementation to work properly, says Paul Barker, executive director of the Institute of National Affairs. Barker said monitoring was about checking whether government policies, plans and projects were actually being carried out and whether they were delivering results.
item.content:encoded:
MONITORING is an important part of government but it must be linked to planning and implementation to work properly, says Paul Barker, executive director of the Institute of National Affairs.

Barker said monitoring was about checking whether government policies, plans and projects were actually being carried out and whether they were delivering results.

“Monitoring and evaluation should provide regular feedback into the planning process so that problems are identified early and corrected, and poor performance is not allowed to continue,” he said.

Barker was responding to the shifting of certain functions from the Department of National Planning and Monitoring to the National Monitoring and Coordination Authority (NMCA).

They include monitoring Public Investment Programme (PIP) projects, coordinating development projects, tracking progress, and carrying out field inspections and compliance checks.

National Planning now focuses mainly on policy, planning, and budgeting.

Barker said the results of monitoring should not be kept within government but made public and used to fix problems and improve service delivery.

He warned that creating or separating institutions without proper coordination could add to Papua New Guinea’s already crowded bureaucracy, instead of solving long-standing problems in how government delivered services.

“The way the National Monitoring and Coordination Authority is structured will determine whether it strengthens accountability or simply becomes another layer in a system already struggling with poor implementation and weak follow-through,” he said.

Barker said monitoring should be done by professional officers working independently, but still closely with planners so decisions can be improved and implementation strengthened.

Meanwhile, Barker said the Department of Prime Minister and National Executive Council already played a key coordination role in government, including through the Central Agency Coordination Committee, which checked NEC submissions to make sure they aligned with government policy and the national budget.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Teaching Service Commission launches website, plans
item.description: THE Teaching Service Commission (TSC) has launched its first website and two strategic plans, marking a significant shift from paper-based record-keeping to digital governance. TSC chairman Maini Ugaia stressed the necessity of the move during the launching yesterday in Port Moresby.
item.content:encoded:
THE Teaching Service Commission (TSC) has launched its first website and two strategic plans, marking a significant shift from paper-based record-keeping to digital governance.

TSC chairman Maini Ugaia stressed the necessity of the move during the launching yesterday in Port Moresby.

“Digitising the TSC is a major step towards better governance,” he said.

The move aims to provide more than 70,000 teachers around the country direct access to essential resources without the need to travel.

Educators can download appointment and leave forms and review policies and circulars from their own communities.

“By moving records online, the Government can identify ghost names, clean the payroll, and stop the waste of public funds,” Ugaia added. 

The TSC also introduced its corporate and teaching service development plans.

The corporate plan targets institutional strengthening, while the development plan outlines strategies to upskill the workforce and ensure that teaching remains a prestigious career. 

Minister for Higher Education, Research, Science and Technology, Kinoka Feo, also the acting Education Minister, emphasised the urgent need for the reforms to improve student outcomes.

Feo revealed that of the 37,000 students enrolled in Grade One, only seven per cent reached tertiary institutions.

“It is a big challenge for all of us to turn the statistics around,” he said. 

Feo also said that teachers should be paid more. 

“Teachers would be paid the highest among all public servants, but that comes with responsibility,” he said.

“If you show the right character, show commitment and dedication at work, you stay.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Move to filter 9,000-plus websites
item.description: MORE than 9,000 Uniform Resource Locators (URL), classified by the Censorship Office and submitted to the National Information and Communications Technology Authority (Nicta) in 2024 and 2025 are to be filtered, an official says. Nicta has been given 30 days by the Government to block “unsuitable internet contents harmful to minors and vulnerable groups”.
item.content:encoded:
MORE than 9,000 Uniform Resource Locators (URL), classified by the Censorship Office and submitted to the National Information and Communications Technology Authority (Nicta) in 2024 and 2025 are to be filtered, an official says.

Nicta has been given 30 days by the Government to block “unsuitable internet contents harmful to minors and vulnerable groups”.

Acting Information and Communications Technology Minister Peter Tsiamalili Jr gave the censorship office and Nicta 30 days to block harmful sites on the internet.

Chief Censor Jim Abani said that Nicta and the Censorship Office had been sharing information through a Google drive-shared folder.

“The classification centre with a technical working group set up in 2024 is still operational,” he said.

“The centre was to work with Nicta and other stakeholders to monitor online environment using guidelines to classify internet protocol (IP) addresses or domains.

“With limited office space and resources, our task in classifying unsuitable content continues with our listing of six have been passed on to Nicta to enforce the implementation of the National Executive Council (NEC) decision 265/2023.

“We had meetings with Nicta last week to revisit the inter-agencies technical working committee and improve, strengthen and reorganise the work started two years ago.”

Abani said their monitoring statistics of URLs showed that the majority of the sites remained 80 per cent accessible across the three main mobile networks.

Explicit sexual activity was the largest sub-category with more than 2,000 URLs.

He said technology was here to stay but as responsible government agencies, they needed to protect citizens from harmful content of mass medium of communication.

“Using of technology and adapting the technology in this space is vital for every one of us to connect with others in this global village, but end users must be wise in decision-making to embrace the communication platforms,” Abani said.

“Be responsible users to all forms of technology that are available.”

He added that censorship laws were clear in that possession, production and distribution of pornography in the country was illegal.

Abani welcomed the call by Tsiamalili as timely.

Attempts to get comments from Nicta were unsuccessful.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Prioritising PNG identity. PNG Chiefs reaffirm pledge for local representation
item.description: THE PNG Chiefs have reaffirmed their commitment to building a team with strong Papua New Guinean representation. General manager of football Michael Chammas stressed that the club wants Papua New Guinean players competing overseas to know they remain central to the Chiefs’ long-term vision ahead of their entry into the National Rugby League in 2028.
item.content:encoded:
THE PNG Chiefs have reaffirmed their commitment to building a team with strong Papua New Guinean representation.

General manager of football Michael Chammas stressed that the club wants Papua New Guinean players competing overseas to know they remain central to the Chiefs’ long-term vision ahead of their entry into the National Rugby League in 2028.

“We want this team to have a strong PNG representation,” Chammas said. “That’s the goal, and it’s very important that this team becomes a reflection of Papua New Guinea.

“We want those players to feel they are part of our plans and that they will get every opportunity possible.”

Chammas noted that marquee signing Jarome Luai is a key figure to help guide and strengthen the future squad, but stressed that local identity will remain at the heart of the club’s mission.

He also revealed that the Chiefs’ upcoming trip to England will focus heavily on recruitment, with head coach Willie Peters playing a pivotal role in identifying talent from the Super League competition.

“No one understands the Super League better than Willie because of the success he’s had over there,” Chammas explained. “There are a lot of players in the Super League that we are interested in, whether they are PNG players or not.”

The trip will allow Chiefs officials to meet Peters face-to-face, watch matches together, and assess which players best fit the style and culture the club is determined to build.

“But we want to spend proper time together, watch football, and understand exactly which players fit the way Willie wants the team to play,” Chammas added.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG Chiefs yet to finalise logo, colours
item.description: THE PNG Chiefs are taking extra time to finalise their official club logo and colours, with chief executive officer Lorna McPherson saying the process is more complicated than many people may realise. “We’re working on the logo at the moment and it’s a long process,” McPherson said. “It has to represent what we believe the PNG Chiefs should stand for but, at the same time, it also has to fit withi
item.content:encoded:
THE PNG Chiefs are taking extra time to finalise their official club logo and colours, with chief executive officer Lorna McPherson saying the process is more complicated than many people may realise.

“We’re working on the logo at the moment and it’s a long process,” McPherson said. “It has to represent what we believe the PNG Chiefs should stand for but, at the same time, it also has to fit within the NRL style of branding and logos.”

McPherson explained that the club is working hard to create a logo that not only represents Papua New Guinea proudly, but also meets the branding standards of the National Rugby League.

She said the club is also facing an important decision when it comes to team colours, especially with many Papua New Guineans strongly connected to the nation’s traditional colours. McPherson admitted the discussions around colours have been challenging, as there are many different opinions from fans and stakeholders across the country.

“You look at the national colours and everyone has their own feelings about it,” she said. “We know people are passionate so, whatever decision is made, some people will love it and others may not.”

The club is gathering feedback from different groups to make sure Papua New Guineans have input into the final design.

“We’re working really hard, listening to feedback and consulting with different groups so everybody can have their say,” she said.

McPherson confirmed the club expects to unveil the official logo and colours within the next few months as preparations continue.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Team of four swimmers return from international duties
item.description: A TEAM of four swimmers returned yesterday from international duties representing Papua New Guinea at the 2026 Oceania Championships in Suva, Fiji, from May 8-13. Nicholas Vava and Ashton Nasinom of the Lae Amateur Swimming Club (LASC) along with Sujith Noka and Tehila Vavar of the Boroko Amateur Swimming Club (BASC), debuted at a regional event.
item.content:encoded:
A TEAM of four swimmers returned yesterday from international duties representing Papua New Guinea at the 2026 Oceania Championships in Suva, Fiji, from May 8-13.

Nicholas Vava and Ashton Nasinom of the Lae Amateur Swimming Club (LASC) along with Sujith Noka and Tehila Vavar of the Boroko Amateur Swimming Club (BASC), debuted at a regional event.

Upon their arrival at the Jackson International terminal, the athletes were all smiles stating they managed to achieve their goals which included setting personal bests (PBs) and experience competition against the region’s best swimmers.

The group also noted their strengths and weaknesses and things to improve on in training in order to be more competitive at such major competitions.

The team was led by Olympian Josh Tarere, alongside Thomas Chen and his sister, Joanna Chen, all of whom brought valuable international experience to the squad.

Team manager Jean Valu-Vava commended the swimmers on their performance at the championship and said this was the first time for some of them to swim in an international competition.

She said they performed admirably, managed to control their nerves, and all swam PBs.

“It takes time to build a good team so with the inclusion of our four experienced swimmers based in Australia for training, they’ve joined the team in Fiji and really brought steadiness with a lot of encouraging words to the four debutants, who have also taken away some good pointers from that international event,” Valu-Vava said.

“They’ve seen where they need improvement on from the swims that they’ve done, they’ve also had a feel of the international competition and it’s way more competitive than the local level because in our local competitions they are the top swimmers in their respective strokes but out there it was clear on where they needed to polish up.”

Also part of the team was 15-year-old Roxanne Kirarock, competing in her second international meet, who impressed with confident racing and achieved personal bests across all her events.

The four experienced athletes are based in Queensland and Victoria, where they continue their training and development, with Tarere training on the Gold Coast under a World Aquatics Scholarship programme.

Across the team, performances reflected determination and progress, with PBs highlighting the athletes’ hard work and commitment on the international stage.

The results underscore the continued growth of swimming in PNG, with the team proudly representing their country and delivering outstanding efforts throughout the championships.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: We want to put together a team that can win: Chammas
item.description: THE PNG Chiefs are determined to build a strong and competitive squad, with general manager (football) Michael Chammas declaring the club is focused on winning, not just competing. “We mean business at the Chiefs,” Chammas said.
item.content:encoded:
THE PNG Chiefs are determined to build a strong and competitive squad, with general manager (football) Michael Chammas declaring the club is focused on winning, not just competing.

“We mean business at the Chiefs,” Chammas said.

“We want to put together a team that can compete and win. The people of Papua New Guinea expect that from us.

“We are not here to just make up the numbers.”

Interest from players and managers has surged following the signings of Jarome Luai and Alex Johnston, with many now eager to join the club.

Chammas praised head coach Willie Peters, describing him as “one of the best coaches in the game”, citing his recent successes including multiple titles and a World Club Challenge victory over the NRL premiers Brisbane Broncos.

The club is now focused on finalising assistant coaches and football staff to create the best possible environment for players.

Chammas confirmed announcements are expected soon, with more player signings likely before Nov 1.

He emphasised that something special is being built for the people of Papua New Guinea, with strong leadership and a clear vision guiding the Chiefs’ journey.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: No path for patients to seek treatment overseas
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea needs a clear path for sending sick patients overseas. A mother in Finschhafen went into labour with conjoined twins. The local hospital had one scanner and it was broken. When the babies began to come, the doctors realised something was very wrong.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea needs a clear path for sending sick patients overseas.

A mother in Finschhafen went into labour with conjoined twins. The local hospital had one scanner and it was broken. When the babies began to come, the doctors realised something was very wrong.

They did a C-section. The twins survived.

Not a medical fight. A paperwork fight. A phone call fight. A fight across three countries and many months.

And it showed one simple, painful truth: PNG has no referral pathway for children born with serious conditions that cannot be treated here.

When a baby is born with a heart problem or joined to a twin, there is no clear door to knock on.

What happens instead is that one person steps up and tries to fix it alone.

In the case of the Finschhafen twins, it took one determined operator to make dozens of phone calls, contact the media, reach a Rotarian group that had done a similar operation in Bougainville 27 years ago, and talked to hospitals in Germany and India, all at the same time.

Only when the story appeared on television did anyone step in to help.

Overseas hospitals are right to ask: Who sent this patient? Who is paying? What is the medical handover plan? These are fair questions.

The problem is that PNG has no answers — not because our doctors are not good, but because no one has built the system to answer them.

When there is no system, sick babies wait.

Clear rules on which patients need care PNG cannot provide, such as newborn heart surgery or complex birth conditions.

These rules already exist in other Pacific countries.

Standing arrangements with hospitals in Australia, New Zealand, or India that say: Yes, we will accept these patients, and here is how.

Not a new negotiation every time a baby is born sick.

International organisations which spend large amounts in PNG should answer this question honestly: Does your budget include help for patients whose lives depend on overseas care?

Right now, it is owned by whoever cares enough to try, a pilot, a visiting doctor, or a Rotary group.

These people are extraordinary. But a system cannot depend on extraordinary individuals.

It must work even when those people are not there.

Now what is the simple truth? There is something from this that says it well: “You had what we didn’t have, and we had what you didn’t have, but together we made it work.”

PNG has strong communities, dedicated local staff, and people who know the land and the patients.

International partners have specialist skills, equipment, and money.

But “together we made it work” is not a policy.

It is what happens when there is no policy.

It is not fair to rely on. And it is not what PNG’s mothers and children deserve.

Not just for the cases that make the news.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Stand as Philip Bouraga did
item.description: All heads of government departments and agencies are called on to read the landmark case of Bourage versus Dutton of 1982. In this case, Philip Bouraga – commissioner of police at the time – refused to divulge certain police operational secrets to then-police minister Warren Dutton.
item.content:encoded:
All heads of government departments and agencies are called on to read the landmark case of Bourage versus Dutton of 1982.

In this case, Philip Bouraga – commissioner of police at the time – refused to divulge certain police operational secrets to then-police minister Warren Dutton.

Dutton threatened to fire Bouraga and the latter, a no nonsense commissioned officer from the same colonial heritage as Dutton, stood his ground and off they trotted to the Supreme Court to settle the matter conclusively.

The Supreme Court affirmed the operational independence of the police commissioner from direct political control, establishing for all time that a minister can’t hire, fire, or discipline, a department head.

That precedent covers all department heads and agencies.

The same principle of separation of powers, seen in operation between the executive, legislative and judicial branches of government is applied here.

That is that the political concerns and the bureaucratic operations must be kept separate and independent of each other.

Those separate roles are not silent – they are spelt out in the laws that establish departments and agencies and in the enabling regulations and further clarified in operational manual of the public servants, the General Orders.

Those ensure that the political head is the law and policy maker, the civil servant is the implementer of the laws and policies. The boundaries between the two is clear and legally inviolable.

The Bouraga vs Dutton case should encourage and embolden the heads of departments and agencies to stand up to protect their duties, responsibilities, legal mandate and their charges from usurpation by politicians.

Sadly, usurpation is the order of the day in so many instances at the present time, that is no secret.

Senior civil servants with decades of experience behind them drop everything to attend to a minister’s wishes even when they know the minister has neither experience nor qualification for the job.

Enormous sums of money, allocated for programmes and projects under the budget appropriation are diverted on a political whim for purposes which are clearly illegal.

Millions of Kina are diverted in this manner.

And in all this, the politician is not guilty.

That is because at all material times the public servant is the Section 32 officer, the only person authorised by law to sign the check and spend funds.

It is the public servant who will go to jail if the law catches up to them.

Department heads do hold very powerful positions in government and, as showed by Bouraga, their positions are defensible in court. 

If they can say “No!” to political directions that are deviant and illegal, they would do themselves and this country a tremendous lot of good.

But today, we hardly hear of a case of the sort Bouraga brought to the Supreme Court.

These days, a department head seeks legal interpretation for alleged wrongful dismissal or being dismissed ahead of time or some such trifle.

It shows the huge gulf that has opened up between personal interests and professional duty since the day of that fine police officer Philip Bouraga and today’s department and agency bosses.

Bouraga thought of his duty, stood by his oath of office and defended both; today’s bosses think of rights and duties as those in their own interest.

There are fine individual officers today who operate by the law and on principle, but the landscape is changed.

Those who operate by principle seem few and far in between or they cower in silence and talk in whispers while the politician, emboldened by the silence move easily to consolidate their power and authority.

But be sure that the politician’s hold on power is shaky exactly because it has no legal or operational basis.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Urgent need to create job opportunities
item.description: I AM writing to draw attention to the pressing issue of employment scarcity in the country. As our country continues to develop, the gap between available job opportunities and the growing workforce has become increasingly apparent.
item.content:encoded:
I AM writing to draw attention to the pressing issue of employment scarcity in the country.

As our country continues to develop, the gap between available job opportunities and the growing workforce has become increasingly apparent.

With a rapidly expanding population, particularly among the youth, there is an urgent need to address the limited employment options available.

Despite Papua New Guinea’s rich natural resources and growing economy, many qualified individuals remain without work.

This scarcity of employment opportunities is not only a challenge for those seeking jobs, but also hinders overall economic growth and development.

Furthermore, it contributes to social instability and disappointment, particularly among the youth who are the future of our nation.

To address this issue, a multi-layered approach is needed.

Investment in education and vocational training, particularly in emerging sectors such as renewable energy, agriculture, and technology, is crucial.

Additionally, public-private partnerships should be encouraged to create more job opportunities, especially in local industries that can provide sustainable employment in the long term.

We must also focus on strengthening infrastructure, improving access to resources, and fostering an entrepreneurial ecosystem that empowers local businesses to thrive.

Only through collaboration and strategic planning can we overcome the employment challenges in PNG and build a more prosperous future for all.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Budget must benefit citizens
item.description: WHILE a well-planned budget is essential for guiding economic policy, it is only effective if the figures translate into tangible, measurable improvements that are felt by the common people. In Papua New Guinea, we have seen numerous budgets announced with motivated goals for infrastructure, education, healthcare, and social services.
item.content:encoded:
WHILE a well-planned budget is essential for guiding economic policy, it is only effective if the figures translate into tangible, measurable improvements that are felt by the common people.

In Papua New Guinea, we have seen numerous budgets announced with motivated goals for infrastructure, education, healthcare, and social services.

However, too often, these promises do not materialise in a way that directly benefits the public.

The allocation of funds, though significant on paper, must be carefully monitored and directed towards projects and services that will truly uplift communities and improve the livelihoods of ordinary citizens.

For the budget to have a meaningful impact, we need more transparency and accountability in its implementation.

It is critical that the Government ensures funds are being spent efficiently and that projects are completed on time.

Additionally, citizens must be informed of how the budget translates into actual benefits – whether it’s through improved healthcare facilities, better education, or more accessible infrastructure.

Ultimately, a budget is only as good as the real-world outcomes it delivers.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s frontier judges: peace-building from the bench
item.description: IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges. Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.
item.content:encoded:
IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges.

Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.

The scale of the challenge is hard to overstate. In Hela and Enga – two of the Highlands provinces hardest hit by inter-group conflict – sophisticated weaponry, warlord networks, and overwhelmed police have combined to produce a crisis that conventional state responses have struggled to contain.

Mobile squad deployments, special policing operations and draconian legislative announcements are all being experimented with.

But quietly, on the ground, judges are trying something different.

As reported in one of the daily newspapers on Feb 19, Justice Andrew Kostopolous, the resident National Court Judge in Hela, used a court-sanctioned inquiry into tribal fighting and lawlessness to confront the province’s deepening governance crisis head-on.

In proceedings marked by unusual candour, he issued bans on alcohol and the public carrying of bush knives, ordered the surrender of firearms, and named specific warlords as criminals subject to immediate arrest.

Justice Kostopolous also issued a human rights violation order against opposing factions in a tribal fight near Tari town, and delivered a stern warning that the province was “teetering on the edge of a constitutional crisis”.

“If the police aren’t doing their job, that’s a constitutional crisis,” he said.

“If the executive is not doing its job, that’s a constitutional crisis.”

These provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into “social engineers”.

The Supreme Court has affirmed that the Constitution is to be interpreted broadly, as “goal-oriented, purposive” and “liberal”, not in a “strict, technical and legalistic” manner.

Judges are urged to “think expansively and to be dynamic” and to “use judicial ingenuity to do justice”.

This is an unusual mandate, though not a unique one: judges in South Africa and India operate under similarly interventionist frameworks.

Three constitutional provisions in PNG are central to this logic.

Section 57 empowers the Supreme and National Courts to protect guaranteed rights and freedoms – either on application or on their own initiative.

Under this provision judges have summoned police to investigate alleged rights breaches, order the closure of prisons on humanitarian grounds, and directed law enforcement commissioners on the management of their facilities.

Critically, Section 57(5) extends this power to cases of “actual or imminent infringement”, allowing courts to make anticipatory orders before a rights breach has fully materialised.

Section 22 grants courts the power to enforce constitutional duties and to formulate procedures and mechanisms where existing frameworks are insufficient.

Section 225 obliges state agencies and service providers to actively support constitutional bodies, including the courts themselves.

Taken together, these provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into what legal scholar Bal Kama describes as “social engineers” – actors empowered to identify systemic failures and act to address them.

In Enga, resident judge Justice Graham Ellis has been equally active.

 Last month, he issued a series of orders on his own initiative aimed at stopping tribal fighting in the Laiagam area.

These included a ban on carrying machetes and axes in public, the establishment of police roadblocks in the Mulitaka area, and orders for police to arrest anyone breaching National Court directives.

He explicitly framed these interventions as a matter of human rights enforcement under Section 57 of the Constitution, noting that the fighting affected rights to life, freedom, protection of the law, freedom of assembly, and freedom of movement.

Justice Ellis’s reasoning is worth quoting at length, because it illuminates the distinctive logic that underpins these interventions: “In many parts of the world, judges can do no more than consider the effects of criminal conduct, notably conducting trials after it is alleged that a crime has been committed. However, the inclusion of human rights provisions in the Constitution of PNG enables a judge to take a more active role by making orders, where it is considered appropriate, that can try to stop and even prevent criminal conduct.”

Both judges are thinking systemically, not just case by case.

Rather than waiting for individual matters to come before them and then ruling on them in isolation, they are using the bench as a vantage point from which to map the conditions generating violence – and to issue orders aimed at disrupting them.

This is a direct challenge to what one judge has described to us as the conventional posture of the justice system: “We tend to come in after everything has happened.

“We wait until it has happened and then clean up the mess.”

item.title: Liberty Liko becomes fourth doctor in family
item.description: HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be. “Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”
item.content:encoded:
HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be.

“Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”

Her father is Dr Osbourne Liko, a surgeon and urologist, who also served as Health Secretary and Medical Board chairman.

Her mother Dr. Mary-Julian Baki is a pediatrician who had served in Gulf, Chimbu and Port Moresby for a total of 23 years. She is now in private practice.

Her brother Dr Malachi Liko graduated in 2020 with a Bachelor in Medicine, Bachelor in Surgery (MBBS) degree. He currently works as a general practitioner.

Her first cousin on her father’s side, Dr. Joyce Sonoling, is a psychiatrist.

On top of that, her family’s close friends include Dr Mathias Sapuri and his daughter Dr Yvonne Sapuri.

Liberty, 23, was among 400-plus students who graduated from the University of Papua New Guinea School of Medicine and Health Science last week.

She had her face painted in Kairuku to honor her Mum, who is the first from Waima in Kairuku to become a doctor.

Liberty wore a Morobe bilum and necklace to honor her Morobe heritage and father who is from Pindu in Finschaffen.

She dressed in the same way as her mother when she graduated from UPNG in 1992. It was to honour her and other women choosing the profession.

“Now that my dream is a reality, I am overwhelmed with gratitude, first and foremost, to our ever merciful God, and to my backbone, my parents.

“I am equally thankful to my entire support network as this achievement is just as much theirs as it is mine. I’ve never had to look far for good role models and I aspire to be even half the doctor my parents are.”

Her parents supported her education into university. She also secured a Government scholarship.

She attended primary school in Australia because of her mother’s overseas work attachment. She later attended the Port Moresby Grammar School.

She completed grades seven to 12 at the St Joseph International College in Port Moresby in 2019 before she was accepted into UPNG on an academic excellence scholarship in 2020. But Covid-19 affected her studies. So to get into medical school, she dropped the Science degree and withdrew from UPNG.

She enrolled at the Pacific Adventist University in 2021 for the Science Foundation with the aim of getting into the School of Medicine and Health Sciences. She attended the UPNG medical school from 2022 to 2025.

She will decide what branch of medicine she is to pursue once she completes her residency and internship.

Dad Dr Liko is proud of her achievement.

“Liberty earned her achievements on her own merits unequivocally. In fact, she had an enormous shoe to fill throughout her journey in pursuit of her medical career. Nearly every lecturer knew her, a majority of doctors and nurses knew who she was.

“But she has always been herself – level-headed, cool, calm and collective. She respects everybody despite any opinion they may have on her. She is always considerate of her less fortunate school mates whose parents are not in Port Moresby.

“She earned her MBBS through disciplined study, always have resilient study habits, perseverance and consistent hard work over five demanding years.

“Despite her family’s medical backgrounds and public roles, I’m proud to say that she never sought advantage or special treatment, choosing instead to uphold fairness and independence.”

Mum Dr Baki described Liberty as confident, determined and an intelligent young lady who always strives for perfection.

“She was no dainty girl growing up and always fought with her six older siblings to be heard. I am very proud of her as a mother and a female professional.”

But she knows well what lies ahead of Liberty.

“This is just the beginning of a long journey of sleepless nights, no weekends or public holidays, missing out on family events and sacrifice of personal time. It is a calling to serve.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
